fields.name: Bipolar Disorder
fields.definition: A chronic mental health condition characterized by dramatic shifts in mood, energy, activity levels, and ability to carry out daily tasks. These shifts — between periods of elevated or irritable mood (mania or hypomania) and periods of depressed mood — are more intense than ordinary ups and downs and can last days to weeks. Bipolar disorder can be an important factor in suicide risk, job loss, and family discord. It is a lifelong condition, but effective treatments can help stabilize mood.
fields.example: A person with bipolar disorder may go weeks feeling barely sleeping and starting ambitious projects, then crash into a depression lasting months where getting out of bed feels impossible.

fields.name: Bipolar I Disorder
fields.definition: A type of bipolar disorder defined by at least one manic episode lasting 7 or more days (or of any duration if so severe that hospitalization is required). Depressive episodes typically occur as well, usually lasting at least 2 weeks. Bipolar I can present with or without psychotic features during manic episodes. It does not require a depressive episode for diagnosis, though most people with Bipolar I experience both.
fields.example: A person with Bipolar I goes 10 days sleeping 2 hours per night, spending thousands of dollars on impulse purchases, and believing they have received a special mission — requiring hospitalization.

fields.name: Bipolar II Disorder
fields.definition: A type of bipolar disorder defined by a pattern of depressive episodes and hypomanic episodes, but no full manic episodes. Bipolar II is sometimes incorrectly viewed as 'milder' than Bipolar I — in reality, its depressive episodes can be severe and prolonged, causing significant impairment. The hypomanic episodes are less extreme than mania and do not require hospitalization, but the overall illness course can be debilitating.
fields.example: A person with Bipolar II has weeks of unusually high energy and productivity followed by months of crushing depression, but never experiences the full severity of a manic episode.

fields.name: Cyclothymic Disorder (Cyclothymia)
fields.definition: A milder but chronic form of bipolar disorder characterized by recurrent hypomanic and depressive symptoms that do not meet full criteria for a hypomanic episode or major depressive episode. Symptoms must persist for at least 2 years in adults (1 year in children). Cyclothymia causes significant instability in mood but without the extremes of full mania or major depression. People with cyclothymia are at increased risk of developing Bipolar I or II.
fields.example: A person cycles between periods of elevated mood and increased productivity and periods of low mood and fatigue that never reach the severity of mania or major depression, but have persisted for 3 years.

fields.name: Manic Episode
fields.definition: A distinct period of abnormally and persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood and increased goal-directed activity or energy, lasting at least 7 days (or any duration if hospitalization is required). During mania, a person may experience: inflated self-esteem or grandiosity, decreased need for sleep (feeling rested after 3 hours), racing thoughts, rapid or pressured speech, distractibility, increased risky behavior (spending sprees, hypersexuality, poor business decisions), and sometimes psychosis.
fields.example: Staying awake for 4 days straight while launching three business plans simultaneously, feeling invincible and certain of success.

fields.name: Hypomanic Episode
fields.definition: A period of elevated, expansive, or irritable mood and increased energy that is clearly different from usual behavior but less severe than a full manic episode. Hypomania lasts at least 4 consecutive days, does not require hospitalization, does not involve psychosis, and does not cause the extreme impairment of mania. Many people describe hypomanic periods as productive and pleasant — which can make Bipolar II harder to identify and lead to delayed diagnosis.
fields.example: A week of needing only 5 hours of sleep, feeling unusually creative and confident, talking more than usual — noticeable to others but not disruptive to functioning.

fields.name: Mixed Features (Bipolar Disorder)
fields.definition: A state in which a person experiences symptoms of both mania/hypomania and depression simultaneously or in rapid alternation. Mixed features can occur during a manic, hypomanic, or depressive episode. This state is particularly dangerous because the elevated energy of mania combines with the hopelessness of depression, increasing the risk of suicidal behavior. Recognizing mixed features changed the DSM-5 classification of bipolar disorder.
fields.example: Feeling deeply hopeless and worthless while simultaneously unable to stop moving, having racing thoughts, and feeling intensely agitated.

fields.name: Mood Stabilizers for Bipolar Disorder
fields.definition: Medications that reduce the frequency and severity of mood episodes in bipolar disorder. Lithium (a naturally occurring salt) is the most established mood stabilizer and has been shown to reduce both manic and depressive episodes, as well as significantly decrease suicide risk. Valproate (valproic acid) is effective particularly for manic episodes and rapid cycling. Lamotrigine is especially useful for preventing depressive episodes. These medications typically require ongoing blood monitoring and take weeks to reach therapeutic effect.
fields.example: A person with Bipolar I starts lithium and, after 6 weeks at therapeutic levels, experiences their first stable stretch in years without a mood episode.

fields.body: Lithium stabilizes mood by influencing multiple neurotransmitter systems — including serotonin, dopamine, and glutamate — and by modulating intracellular signaling pathways involved in neuroplasticity. It requires reaching and maintaining a specific therapeutic blood concentration (typically 0.6–1.2 mEq/L), which is monitored with regular blood tests. It also protects neurons and promotes growth factors in the brain. Beyond mood stabilization, lithium has the strongest evidence of any psychiatric medication for reducing suicide risk — studies suggest it can reduce suicide deaths by approximately 60% in people with bipolar disorder. However, lithium has a narrow therapeutic window and requires careful dosing.
fields.name: How Lithium Works
fields.example: A patient started on lithium has blood drawn regularly to ensure the dose is in the therapeutic range, avoiding both underdosing (ineffective) and toxicity (dangerous).

fields.question: How many US adults have bipolar disorder?
fields.answer: An estimated 2.8% of US adults had bipolar disorder in the past year, based on data from the National Comorbidity Survey Replication. Prevalence is similar across males (2.9%) and females (2.8%). Bipolar I and bipolar II each occur in approximately 1% of the population; bipolar spectrum disorder (including subthreshold conditions) is estimated to affect roughly 2.4–4% of the population.

fields.question: What is the average age of onset for bipolar disorder?
fields.answer: Most bipolar disorder symptoms begin during late adolescence or early adulthood. The average age of first episode is approximately 25, though onset can occur in the teens and, less commonly, in childhood. Earlier age of onset is associated with more severe illness course and higher risk of comorbidities.

fields.question: How does bipolar disorder relate to suicide risk?
fields.answer: Bipolar disorder is associated with significantly elevated suicide risk. People with bipolar disorder — particularly during depressive or mixed-feature episodes — are at higher risk for suicidal thoughts and attempts than the general population. Lithium treatment has the strongest evidence for reducing suicide risk in bipolar disorder, estimated to reduce suicide deaths by approximately 60%. If you or someone you know is in crisis, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline by calling or texting 988.

fields.question: What is the key difference between Bipolar I and Bipolar II?
fields.answer: The key difference is the presence and severity of elevated-mood episodes. Bipolar I requires at least one full manic episode (lasting 7+ days or requiring hospitalization). Bipolar II requires at least one hypomanic episode (at least 4 days, less severe than full mania, no hospitalization required) and at least one major depressive episode, but no full manic episode. Bipolar II is not simply a milder version of Bipolar I — its depressive burden can be equally severe.

fields.name: Psychotherapy Approaches for Bipolar Disorder
fields.definition: Several evidence-based therapies are used alongside medication for bipolar disorder. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) helps identify and change negative thought patterns and develop coping strategies. Interpersonal and Social Rhythm Therapy (IPSRT) focuses on stabilizing daily routines and sleep-wake cycles, which helps regulate mood cycles. Family-Focused Therapy improves communication and problem-solving within families, reducing relapse risk. Research shows patients who receive intensive psychotherapy alongside medication recover faster and maintain stability longer than those receiving medication alone.
fields.example: A person with Bipolar II works with a therapist using IPSRT to establish a consistent sleep schedule and daily routine, reducing the frequency of hypomanic episodes triggered by disrupted rhythms.

fields.question: Isn't bipolar disorder just having strong mood swings — doesn't everyone experience those?
fields.answer: No. NIMH directly addresses this: while "everyone experiences ups and downs, with bipolar disorder, the range of mood changes can be extreme." Manic episodes involve markedly elevated or irritable mood and dramatically increased activity that lasts most of the day, every day — for at least 7 days, or any duration if hospitalization is required. These episodes cause changes in behavior that are noticeable to others and significantly impair functioning or require medical intervention. This is qualitatively different from the normal emotional variation everyone experiences. Bipolar disorder is a serious, treatable medical condition — not a personality trait or dramatic disposition.
